title: Finding Your Pain Point
description: Your best tool idea is hiding in your own daily frustrations.

learningObjectives.text: Identify problems in your daily workflow worth automating
Explain why personal pain points make better ideas than brainstormed ones
List 3 potential tool ideas from your own experience

contentSections.body: Every great tool started as someone saying 'I can't believe I have to do this manually.' Zapier, Notion, Linear — all started because their founders were annoyed. Your annoyance is your product.

The best tool ideas don't come from brainstorming. They come from **your own pain**.

Every day, you probably:
- Do something repetitive that takes 30 minutes and could take 30 seconds
- Switch between tools that should talk to each other but don't
- Format data by hand that could be formatted automatically
- Look up the same things repeatedly

Those are ideas. Write them down.

contentSections.question: Marcus is a salesperson who spends 2 hours/week manually copying data from his CRM into email templates. Should he build a tool for this?
contentSections.answer: **Yes — classic pain point.** Repetitive, time-consuming, high personal value, and almost certainly shared by every salesperson who uses that CRM. His first user is himself. Validation is instant.
contentSections.hint: Think: does he already know the problem is real?

contentSections.body: The best tool idea is one where your first user is yourself and your first test is tomorrow.

### The pain point formula

> "Every time I [do X], I have to [tedious thing]. I wish I could just [outcome]."

Examples:
- "Every time I get a PDF, I have to manually extract the tables. I wish I could just paste a link and get a spreadsheet."
- "Every time I write a PR description, I have to look up the ticket. I wish git commit messages auto-populated the description."
- "Every time I prepare for a meeting, I spend 10 minutes googling the person. I wish it just summarized their LinkedIn automatically."

Each of these is a real tool someone has built (and charged for).
**Energy constraint note (Sarah):** You don't need to have a big idea. You need *a* idea. A tool that saves you 5 minutes/day across 200 workdays saves you 1,000 minutes per year — 16+ hours back.

Right now, write down 3 tasks you do manually that feel automatable. Don't judge them. Just list them.

Good candidates:
- Anything you do more than once a week
- Anything that involves copy-paste between tools
- Anything that requires looking something up every time
- Anything where you follow the exact same steps in the exact same order

Bad candidates:
- "A platform where everyone can..." (too big)
- "An AI that does everything" (too vague)
- "A social network for [niche]" (too complex)

**Alex's shortlist:** (1) Auto-summarize meeting recordings → bullet points. (2) Generate weekly progress reports from Jira tickets. (3) Convert Slack threads into docs. All three are real, 2-hour builds.

title: Validate Before You Build
description: 5 conversations beat 500 hours of coding on the wrong thing.

learningObjectives.text: Define what validation means for a solo tool builder
Conduct a 5-minute user conversation to test an idea
Distinguish between polite validation and genuine demand

contentSections.body: The number one way to waste 3 months of effort: build something nobody wanted. The number one way to prevent it: talk to 5 people before writing a single line of code.

Validation sounds like a startup buzzword. For solo tool builders, it's simpler:

**Ask 5 people:** "Do you ever deal with [the problem I'm solving]?"

If 4 out of 5 say "oh god yes" — you have an idea worth building.
If 4 out of 5 shrug — go back to your list.

You're not running a survey. You're looking for emotional resonance.

contentSections.question: You ask 5 colleagues if they struggle with manually formatting weekly reports. 4 say 'yeah, it's annoying but whatever.' 1 says 'I HATE that, I spend 3 hours every Friday on it.' Which response matters more?
contentSections.answer: **The passionate 1.** The 4 'yeah whatever' responses show awareness of the problem. The 1 who hates it shows *pain intensity*. Build for the person who hates the problem, not the person who's merely aware of it.
contentSections.hint: Strong emotion is a signal. Mild annoyance might not be enough to make someone pay or change habits.

contentSections.body: Validate by looking for pain intensity, not just problem awareness. 'Yeah, that's annoying' ≠ 'I would pay to fix that.'

### The 3-question script

1. "Do you ever deal with [problem]?" → Listen for frustration level
2. "What do you do about it now?" → Current workaround = confirmed problem
3. "How long does it take / how often does it happen?" → Quantify the pain

**Do NOT say:** "I'm building a tool for this. Would you use it?" — people say yes to be nice.

**DO listen for:** They describe their workaround in detail. They ask 'when will it be ready?' without being prompted. They name others who have the same problem.

**The politeness trap:** People are kind. They'll say "sure, I'd probably use it" even if they wouldn't. Look for behavior, not promises. Has the person ever paid for a similar tool? Do they currently spend time solving this manually?

After 5 conversations, you should know:

**Green light — build it:**
- At least 3/5 have the problem
- At least 1/5 has strong emotional pain about it
- Someone asked when it'll be ready

**Pivot — rethink:**
- Fewer than 3/5 have the problem
- Everyone's workaround is 'good enough'
- No one expressed urgency

**Note:** If YOU have the problem and would use the tool daily, that's already 1 green light. Build for yourself first.
**Solo builder advantage:** You don't need a market. You need *users*. 10 people who love a tool you built in a weekend is a win.

title: Scope It Down: The MVP Mindset
description: The smallest version that proves your idea works — that's what you build first.

learningObjectives.text: Define MVP (Minimum Viable Product) in your own words
Apply scope-reduction to a tool idea
Design a 2-hour first version of a tool

contentSections.body: You don't need to build Notion. You need to build the thing that proves the idea works. Then you can add features. This week, the goal is: does the core thing work?

**MVP = Minimum Viable Product.**

Not minimum *finished* product. Not minimum *polished* product.
Minimum *viable* — does it do the one thing that proves the value?

### What counts as viable?
- Does it solve the core problem?
- Would you use it in its current form?
- Could you show it to someone and explain the value in 30 seconds?

If yes → it's viable. Add features later.

contentSections.question: Alex wants to build a meeting summarizer. His v1 plan: transcribe audio, summarize, extract action items, email attendees, integrate with calendar. Is this an MVP?
contentSections.answer: **No — that's a v3.** The MVP is: paste a transcript → get a summary. One input, one output. Once that works and people use it, add transcription. Then action items. Then email. Never build features before proving the core.
contentSections.hint: Which single step delivers the most value if everything else is manual?

contentSections.body: Every feature you don't build in v1 is a feature you don't have to debug, maintain, or explain.

### Scope to 2 hours

For every tool idea, ask: **"What's the version I can build in 2 hours?"**

This forces you to:
- Drop the 'nice to haves'
- Focus on the single core action
- Use Claude to write the boilerplate
- Skip authentication, databases, and UI polish

A 2-hour version is just:
- 1 input (paste text / upload file / enter URL)
- 1 Claude API call
- 1 output (text in the terminal or on a page)

That's it. That's your v1.

**Marcus's meeting summarizer v1:**
```
$ node summarize.js meeting_transcript.txt
> Summary: discussed Q3 goals, action items: [list]
```
No UI. No database. No auth. Works in 2 hours. Proves the idea. Then he adds a web UI.

Write a 3-line spec for your tool:

```
Tool name: [name]
Input: [what the user provides]
Output: [what the tool returns]
```

That's your entire v1 spec. If you can't fit it in 3 lines, scope it down more.

### Examples:
- **PR Describe:** Input: git diff. Output: Pull request description.
- **PDF Data:** Input: PDF URL. Output: CSV of tables.
- **Reply Suggest:** Input: email text. Output: 3 reply options.
**Next lesson preview:** You'll build your first CLI tool using exactly this spec. Your 3-line spec becomes your first Node.js script.
